turns.content: I'm following this talk on async databases, but I still don't get why we even need an entity derive macro for things like has_many or belongs_to. Isn't just a table column enough?

That is a common frustration when moving from raw SQL to an ORM. The entity derive macro isn't just syntactic sugar; it's the mechanism that allows SeaORM to understand your schema relationships at compile time, enabling traits like HasMany and BelongsTo which you cannot replicate with plain column definitions alone.
So I can just write a struct with #[derive(Entity)] and it will magically know how to join tables? That seems too simple for what looks like complex database logic.

It is surprisingly straightforward once you define the relationships. When you use #[derive(Entity)], SeaORM generates a crate of types that handle the SQL generation, so your struct becomes an actual entity type that knows how to map to the database.
Okay, but what about async? I thought we had to write raw queries if we want async behavior?

In SeaORM, you never write raw SQL for these operations. You use the async trait on your connection pool and then call methods like find_with_related() which handles the async database interaction internally.
Wait, I thought find_with_related was just a query builder pattern? Why do I need to explicitly call that method instead of using normal queries?

find_with_related is the specific async method designed to fetch related entities in a single round-trip. It avoids N+1 problems by eagerly loading the connections defined in your entity struct, which is much more efficient than chaining multiple queries.
I see. So if I have a User entity with has_many(posts), calling find_with_related(User) will automatically include all posts?
Exactly. It executes one transaction or query set that pulls the user and their associated posts together, returning a tuple of (User, Vec<Post>) instead of separate lists.
That sounds great, but what if I want to filter those posts? Can find_with_related handle WHERE clauses?
Yes. You can pass a predicate function to find_with_related that filters the results before they are returned, so you get only the users who have active posts.
I'm new to open source contributions and I have a question about how SeaORM handles relations. Basically, does it just use standard SQL JOINs or is there something else going on under the hood?

SeaORM doesn't rely on raw SQL joins for relations; instead, you define your relationships using the Entity derive macro on your structs. When you annotate a field with @has_many or @belongs_to, SeaORM generates the necessary association logic automatically so you don't have to write manual join queries.

That sounds convenient but I'm confused about the actual data flow. If I query a User entity that has many Posts, does SeaORM actually fetch all those posts in one go or do I have to write explicit loops?

By default, when you call methods like find() on an Entity, it only loads the primary entity and leaves related tables untouched unless you explicitly request them. To get the related Posts with a single query, you use find_with_related which constructs a complex SELECT statement that joins your User table with the Post table based on the foreign key defined in the schema.

Okay so I can do this: let users = db.find().with_relations(Post::Entity).execute()?; Does that mean it returns a Vector of User entities where each User already contains its own vector of Post references?

Exactly. The find_with_related method returns an EntityResult which holds the list of users, and because we requested relations, every user struct in that collection will have the related posts attached as Vecs inside them without any further database calls.
Wait, I thought maybe there was a simpler way. Can't I just write a raw query with JOINs and let SeaORM execute it directly?

You can definitely write raw queries using the QueryBuilder or your database driver's native API, but that bypasses the ORM abstraction. If you want to stay within the typed ecosystem where you get compile-time safety and automatic schema generation without manually writing join clauses every time, you should use the Entity derive macros with has_many and belongs_to annotations instead.
I see. But what if I want to load only specific fields from those related tables? Like just the title of each post but not the full Post struct?

You can still use find_with_related but you'll need to select specific columns or use projection macros when defining your Entity structs. Alternatively, if you really only want a subset of data and don't care about full type safety, you could manually construct the query using Column::Name references combined with SelectBuilder.
Hmm so it seems like I'm stuck between doing manual SQL queries or using Entity derive macros. Is there a middle ground?

The middle ground is leveraging the relational types that SeaORM provides. You don't have to manually write JOINs if you use the RelationType enum and let the ORM handle the query building for you while still having access to specific columns through the generated types.
Okay I think I understand now. So instead of raw SQL, I define my User struct with @has_many(Post::Entity) and then call find_with_related()? That's what I should do for async CRUD operations?

Yes, that is exactly the right approach. You define your entities using derive macro annotations to declare relationships, then use methods like find(), insert(), update() on those Entity types which handle all the async database interactions and relation loading automatically.

Wait hold on earlier you mentioned I could write raw queries but now you're saying I should avoid them. But what if my schema changes frequently? Wouldn't manually managing joins be more flexible than relying on derive macros?

That's a valid concern and actually the main reason SeaORM exists. The Entity derive approach keeps your code decoupled from raw SQL syntax so you don't have to manually maintain join logic when adding new columns or tables. However, if you absolutely need fine-grained control over which columns are selected without loading entire structs, then yes you would fall back on manual query builders.

I'm trying to port our legacy SQL project to Rust using SeaORM, but I keep getting confused about how the entity derive macro handles relations. Specifically, does it automatically generate the necessary traits for async queries or do I need to manually implement them? I've seen code where `find_with_related` returns a future, so I assume the magic happens in the derive macro itself.

The confusion is understandable because the derive macro generates the core entity types and implements the necessary traits like `EntityTrait` for you, but it doesn't handle async execution directly. When you define an Entity with relations using the derive macro, SeaORM automatically implements the trait that allows you to call methods like `find_with_related`. However, these methods return futures because Rust's async/await system requires them to be asynchronous by default. You don't need to manually implement any traits; the macro handles generating the implementation of the trait for your Entity type based on the relation definitions you provide in the derive call.

Okay, so I just drop down `#[derive(Entity)]` and SeaORM does all the heavy lifting? But wait, what if my table has a many-to-many relationship like users and posts? Does that change how I implement the trait or do I need extra boilerplate code to handle the join queries manually?

You don't need extra boilerplate for most common relationships including many-to-many, as long as you define them correctly in the derive macro. For a many-to-many relation like users and posts, you simply declare the `Many` attribute with an optional key column specification if needed. The macro will then generate the necessary code to handle the join logic internally when you call methods on the Entity. However, be aware that for complex queries involving joins across multiple tables or custom filtering conditions, you might need to compose these generated methods into larger async functions yourself rather than relying solely on a single method from the trait.

That makes sense about composition, but I'm still stuck on one thing. When I use `find_with_related` with eager loading, does it actually fetch all the related rows in one database call or does it spawn multiple async tasks under the hood? I want to avoid N+1 query problems and ensure my application stays performant as we scale.

The behavior of `find_with_related` depends entirely on how you specify the eager loading parameters. If you pass a list of relation names to the method, it will generate a single SQL query with multiple JOINs to fetch all related rows in one database call, which is exactly what you want to avoid N+1 problems. However, if you omit those parameters or use different methods like `find` without specifying relations, SeaORM might issue separate queries for each relation found in your Entity definition. You should always explicitly list the eager-loaded relations when performance matters to guarantee a single round-trip query.

I see now that explicit parameter passing is key here. But I'm wondering about error handling too. Since these methods return futures, how do I properly handle errors from the database connection and ensure my async functions don't panic if the connection fails? Should I be using Result types or something else entirely?

You should definitely use Result types to propagate errors through your async chains. The methods on the Entity trait, including `find_with_related`, return futures that eventually resolve to a Result containing either the data or an error variant. You can then call `.await` on these futures within your function and match against the Result to handle connection failures gracefully without panicking. This pattern keeps your code idiomatic by avoiding try-except blocks and instead using explicit error handling with the standard Rust Result type throughout your async workflow.

Got it, so I'm building a service that fetches users with their posts. I need to make sure my service function properly awaits the future returned by `find_with_related`. But what if I want to filter this data later? Can I chain operations or do I have to create separate queries for filtering?

You can absolutely chain operations on the same Entity type, but you must be careful about when eager loading happens versus when filtering occurs. If you call `find_with_related` first and then try to filter the resulting collection, you'll need to perform a second database round-trip unless you've already applied filters in your initial query using the builder pattern provided by SeaORM's async methods. The most efficient approach is to apply all your WHERE clause conditions directly within the `find_with_related` call itself before awaiting it, so you get filtered and eager-loaded data in a single database interaction.

Perfect, that clarifies the order of operations. One last thing though - I'm curious about the difference between using Entity traits versus the Table trait. When should I use which? Are there scenarios where one is strictly better than the other for managing these relations and async queries?

The Entity trait is generally preferred for most CRUD operations involving relations because it encapsulates both the table metadata and relation definitions in a single interface, making it easier to manage complex associations like many-to-many or self-references. The Table trait focuses primarily on raw column access without relation logic, so it's useful when you need low-level control over specific columns but don't care about automatic join handling. For your use case with user posts and eager loading, the Entity trait is strictly better because it handles the complexity of generating correct JOIN clauses automatically.

I'm preparing for a technical interview at my startup, and I have a hypothetical scenario: we need to fetch a user record along with their complete list of posts in a single database call. How do I handle this async relationship in SeaORM?

In your scenario, you are defining the User entity struct using derive macros from sea_orm_entity and then implementing the has_many relation on that User type to link it to the Post table. The core mechanism for fetching this data together is the find_with_related method which generates a single SQL JOIN query instead of separate round trips.
That sounds efficient, but I'm worried about performance if we have many posts. Does the eager loading limit how many rows it can actually fetch from the database in one go?

The find_with_related method does execute a single query with a JOIN clause, but you are correct to question the scaling; while the SQL is efficient, loading too many related records can cause memory pressure. A better approach for large datasets might be using a cursor-based pagination on the collection returned by find_with_related or implementing lazy loading where you only fetch posts when they are accessed.
Ah, so I should avoid eager loading for massive tables? But what if I still want to keep it simple and just need a basic implementation of async CRUD operations?

For a basic implementation, you can define your User entity with the derive macros from sea_orm_entity and then use the sea_orm::EntityTrait to perform async inserts and selects. When you need relations, you create a separate Post entity struct and implement a belongs_to relation on that table back to User, allowing you to query posts by user_id efficiently.

I think I'm getting confused about the direction of the relationship. Is it always has_many from parent to child?

Wait, I think I misunderstood the schema earlier. So if User has_many Post, then Post must have a user_id column? That means I should be using find_with_related on the User entity to get posts, not on the Post entity.

Exactly, you are correct; that is the critical insight. You fetch the User record and its associated Posts in one call by calling find_with_related on the User entity itself, passing the Post entity as the relation argument to the method.

Hey, I'm trying to build an automation script for our CI/CD pipeline but I keep getting confused about how SeaORM entities differ from just using raw SQL queries. Is the main difference that entities are synchronous while raw queries are async?

Not quite, that's a common misconception. Entities in SeaORM aren't inherently synchronous or asynchronous; they're just type-safe wrappers around your database schema definitions. You can use them with either sync or async drivers depending on what you need. The real benefit is how they handle relations like has_many and belongs_to automatically. For example, if you define a User entity that has_many Posts, SeaORM will generate the necessary join logic for you instead of writing complex raw SQL.

Ah okay so I don't have to manually write JOIN clauses? That sounds much easier than managing raw queries. But wait, does that mean I can't still use async if my tool needs to fetch many records efficiently?

Exactly right! You absolutely can and should use async for performance. SeaORM provides an async CRUD interface through traits like BoxedAsyncCrudTrait that you implement on your entities. When you call methods like find_with_related, it automatically uses async execution under the hood if your driver supports it (like tokio). This is much better than synchronous queries when dealing with large datasets or complex relationships.

Got it. So for my automation tool I should probably just define entities and use find_with_related instead of writing raw joins? But what about eager loading vs lazy loading - do entities force one approach?

Entities give you both options. If you want eager loading where all related records are fetched in a single query, use find_with_related on your entity methods. That's essentially what makes the entity pattern so powerful - it abstracts away whether you're doing lazy or eager loading at the query level while keeping type safety. For async operations specifically, the builder patterns help you compose these queries without manual SQL construction.

That makes sense. But I'm still worried about performance - if my tool fetches thousands of users with their posts, will find_with_related actually be faster than running separate queries for each user?

Yes significantly! That's the whole point. A single query fetching all users with their posts is vastly more efficient than N+1 queries where you fetch one user then loop through to get each of their posts. The async nature means you can also batch these operations nicely with tokio, so your automation script won't block while waiting for database responses.
Perfect! One last thing though - if I'm building this as a CLI tool that needs to run synchronously sometimes, can entities still work without async?

You can absolutely. SeaORM supports both sync and async modes through different trait implementations. You'd use the BoxedCrudTrait for synchronous operations instead of the async variants. The entity definitions themselves remain identical - only the query execution method changes based on whether you're in sync or async mode.

I've been using Java for years, so I know the pain of writing raw SQL. I want to write async CRUD in Rust with SeaORM but I'm overwhelmed by the boilerplate. How do I actually define an entity and handle relations without getting lost in syntax?

The core pattern is using derive macros on structs, like #[derive(SeaOrmEntity)] for your table schema. For relationships you use traits like HasMany or BelongsTo to declare the foreign key constraints.
That sounds generic. I need something concrete. If I have a User and a Post, how do I define that a user has many posts without manually writing join conditions every time?

You define the relation on the structs themselves using #[derive(HasMany)] or #[derive(BelongsTo)]. For example, your User struct derives HasMany with a Post entity type, and your Post struct derives BelongsTo referencing the same User. This tells SeaORM exactly which columns to join.
Okay, so I can just drop all that manual SQL writing? But what if I need to query with filters or order by specific columns in the result?

You still build your queries against the entity types you defined. You use methods like .where() and .order() on your selected fields, but SeaORM generates the correct SQL under the hood based on those relations.
I'm struggling with eager loading though. I keep getting N+1 queries when doing async CRUD. How do I use find_with_related to fetch related posts in a single query?
Use the .find_with_related() method on your entity type, passing the relation you defined earlier as an argument. This forces SeaORM to emit a JOIN instead of separate queries for each user.
Wait, I thought find_with_related only worked with sync queries? Is this actually async or did I just get it wrong?

SeaORM supports both sync and async versions of that method. The async variant returns a Future that resolves to your entities plus the related ones, so yes, you can absolutely use find_with_related in async contexts.
Okay, so I just call .find_with_relation::<Post>(self)? But what if I want to load nested relations like comments on posts?
You simply chain the relation types. If a Post has Many Comments, you can pass HasMany<Comment> as another argument to find_with_relation so SeaORM builds a multi-table join.
This is getting complicated. Is there any way to avoid this complexity and keep the code simple?

The best practice is to always define your relations explicitly at compile time using derive macros rather than guessing. Once you have those traits set up, find_with_related becomes straightforward because SeaORM knows exactly what joins to generate.
